2024 · arXiv · Post-training & RL

Revisiting the Superficial Alignment Hypothesis

LIMA argued that post-training is mostly cosmetic — a thousand examples to teach a base model the right style. Run the experiment on objective benchmarks instead of win-rates and a different picture appears: task accuracy scales as a power law in the number of finetuning examples, driven by reasoning, not formatting.

♪ listen — audio episode
3-voice deep dive · mp3 ↗ · rss feed ↗
Mohit Raghavendra, Vaskar Nath, Sean Hendryx · Scale AI / Georgia Tech
venue arXiv citations 13 topic Post-training & RL source ↗ listen ♪
// citation count as of 2026-06
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

The Superficial Alignment Hypothesis, popularized by LIMA (Zhou et al., 2023), is one of the most consequential folk-theorems in post-training. It bundles three claims: (C1) a model's knowledge is learned entirely during pre-training; (C2) a small number of examples can saturate a model's performance on a task; and (C3) post-training is largely about style and formatting, and does not teach new capabilities. If true, the practical implication is enormous and seductive: stop collecting data. A thousand curated prompts is all you need, and any spend beyond that is waste.

The trouble is how that hypothesis was measured. LIMA and its follow-ups evaluate chatbot-style, open-ended prompts using subjective win-rate — pit two models head-to-head and ask a judge (human or LLM) which response it prefers. That protocol rewards tone, fluency, and format, exactly the cosmetic surface the hypothesis is about. It says almost nothing about whether the model can actually do mathematics, write correct code, or chain a multi-step inference. For the practitioner who finetunes an LLM to solve a real task, the question that matters — "does my benchmark number go up if I collect 10× more data?" — was never the question LIMA answered.

This paper re-runs the experiment with the missing instrument: standardized, objective, task-specific benchmarks across math, coding, instruction-following, and multi-hop QA, while varying the amount of finetuning data from 0 to 10,000 examples and the model size from 7B to 70B. The goal is to characterize the actual shape of post-training scaling, and to ask whether the three claims survive contact with metrics that can't be gamed by style.

// how it works

The method

The setup is a controlled data-scaling sweep. Starting from base (not instruct) checkpoints — chosen because instruct models are already heavily finetuned on these very tasks — the authors supervised-finetune on dataset splits of 0, 100, 500, 1,000, 5,000 and 10,000 examples (until each training set is exhausted), for 3 epochs, with identical hyperparameters within a model family and the default HuggingFace chat template. Four capabilities are probed, each with a clean train/test separation:

TaskTest benchmarkTrain dataset# train
MathGSM8kGSM8k train7,500
Multihop QnASubQASubQA train2,700
CodingHumanEval+StarCoder Self-Align10,000
Instruction followingIFEvalConifer (hard)5,000
Instruction followingIFEvalDolly-15k15,000

Models span three families and a 10× size range: Llama-3 (8B, 70B), Llama-2 (7B, 13B, 70B), and Mistral-7B. The central empirical claim is that accuracy follows a power law against finetuning-example count — the same functional form as pre-training and inference scaling laws (Kaplan et al., 2020; Brown et al., 2024), but in examples rather than tokens, which is the unit practitioners actually budget and annotate:

P ∝ D1/b // P = task accuracy on the benchmark · D = number of finetuning examples · b = fitted exponent (per-task, per-model coefficients in App. A.6)

To separate style from substance (research question 2), the authors take Llama-3-8B finetuned at 100, 1,000 and full splits on GSM8k (math) and SubQA (multihop) — both of which use a Socratic subquestion-then-answer reasoning format — collect every incorrect response, and have GPT-4o independently label each one as incorrect formatting, incorrect reasoning, or (for math) incorrect arithmetic. Because the categories are scored independently, one wrong answer can fall into several, letting them watch which error class shrinks with more data.

To test whether post-training can teach genuinely new knowledge (research question 3), they build Facts100: 100 hand-curated world events from after Llama-3-8B's March-2023 cutoff (entertainment, sports, business, politics, science), each paired with a single-hop Direct question and a two-hop Multihop question. New knowledge is injected two ways — Event-SFT (finetune on the fact) and RAG-Oracle (place the fact in the prompt with a perfect retriever) — applied to both a plain base model and a model already post-trained for multi-hop reasoning on pre-cutoff data.

// what they found

The results

1. Task accuracy scales as a clean power law — it does not saturate. Across every family and task, accuracy keeps climbing with data following P ∝ D1/b, contradicting C2's "a few examples saturate performance." Scaling is most regular on reasoning-heavy tasks (math, multihop QA, coding) and noisiest on the subjective instruction-following task. Crucially the curves don't cross: a stronger base model is consistently stronger after post-training, which is itself a vote for the importance of pre-training.

2. Bigger models learn more from the same data. Scaling Llama-2 and Llama-3 from 7B to 70B keeps the power-law form, but the gain isn't a flat upward shift — the larger models' curves bend upward, i.e. their rate of improvement per example increases. Pre-training scale doesn't just raise the starting point; it raises the slope of post-training returns.

3. Style saturates fast; reasoning is what keeps improving. The error breakdown is the heart of the paper. Formatting errors do collapse within ~100 examples — exactly as the Superficial Alignment Hypothesis predicts. But total mistakes track reasoning errors almost perfectly and keep falling with more data: on Llama-3-8B the correlation between total mistakes and reasoning errors is r² = 0.98 (math) and 0.99 (multihop), versus only 0.93 / 0.83 for formatting errors. So "a glance says it's aligned" (style fixed at 100 examples) and "it's actually correct" (reasoning improving for thousands) are different events — and the hypothesis conflates them.

4. Win-rate evaluation is actively misleading for reasoning tasks. The authors finetune Llama-3-8B on LIMA's 1,000 prompts (LIMA-1k) versus 1,000 task-specific examples (Task-1k) — same data budget — then score both ways. On GSM8k math, the chatbot-styled LIMA-1k model wins the GPT-4o head-to-head 84.4% of the time despite answering only 14.7% of problems correctly, while the actually-competent Task-1k model (46.5% accuracy) wins just 0.24% of comparisons. A judge optimizing for chatbot polish can crown the worse reasoner.

GSM8k mathTask accuracyWin-rate (GPT-4o)
LIMA-1k (style)14.7%84.4%
Task-1k (task data)46.5%0.24%
Multihop · LIMA-1k21%20%
Multihop · Task-1k36%57%

5. Post-training for reasoning unlocks new, post-cutoff knowledge. On Facts100, both base and reasoning-post-trained models start near-blind (Direct/Multihop accuracy of 8/12 vs 32/27). After injecting the facts, the gap explodes on the multihop question — the one that requires using the new fact, not just reciting it. With RAG-Oracle the reasoning-post-trained model reaches 86 / 71 (Direct/Multihop) versus the base model's 49 / 34; with Event-SFT it's 81 / 55 vs 65 / 37. Reasoning post-training (done entirely on pre-cutoff data) is what lets a model apply knowledge it was handed, and it cuts — though does not eliminate — event hallucination.

// does it hold up?

The eval read

The paper's strongest move is also its most quietly rigorous: it anticipates the obvious objection. The whole power-law story for math could be a GSM8k contamination artifact — base models have seen grade-school math, and finetuning could be surfacing memorized solutions rather than improving reasoning. So Appendix A.1 re-runs the math sweep on GSM1k (Zhang et al., 2024), a held-out parallel benchmark built specifically to detect GSM8k leakage, and the same power-law trend reappears (below). That is the right control, and it materially strengthens the math result.

But the contamination diligence is uneven. GSM1k covers math only; the coding (HumanEval+), instruction-following (IFEval) and multihop (SubQA) curves get no equivalent held-out twin, and HumanEval-family contamination in 2024-era base models is well documented. The headline "power law across capabilities" rests on a contamination control for one of four.

The power-law claim itself is fit to ~5–6 points per curve (the 0/100/500/1k/5k/10k splits), with no goodness-of-fit (R²) reported in the main text, no error bars, and what reads as single-seed training. Over a single order of magnitude of data, a power law, a logarithm, and a slow saturating curve are nearly indistinguishable on six noisy points — so "power law" is better read as "monotone, non-saturating, and roughly linear in log-log" than as a precisely identified exponent. The 70B runs are explicitly sparse ("we limited the number of training runs"), so the most striking claim — that big models' curves bend upward — leans on the fewest data points.

The win-rate demonstration is rhetorically devastating but slightly circular. It uses a single LLM judge (GPT-4o) with LIMA's own prompt to show that LLM judges are fooled by style — true and useful, but a human win-rate study would prove the deeper claim that win-rate misleads people, not just GPT-4o. And the LIMA-1k vs Task-1k contest is a generalist-on-the-specialist's-turf comparison: LIMA was never meant to maximize GSM8k, so its 14.7% isn't a refutation of LIMA's thesis so much as a vivid illustration that task accuracy and chatbot-preference are different axes — which is exactly the (fair) point, as long as it isn't oversold as "LIMA is wrong about alignment."

Finally, scope. Everything here is single-task SFT on text: no RLHF/DPO (the dominant second stage), no multi-task interference or catastrophic-forgetting analysis (the authors flag this as open), and Facts100 is only 100 events scored by GPT-4o without human validation, so its accuracy counts (8, 12, 32…) are small-N and noisy. RAG-Oracle assumes a perfect retriever, inflating absolute numbers relative to any deployed RAG. None of this sinks the paper — the qualitative story (style saturates, reasoning scales, objective benchmarks matter) is robust — but the precise functional form deserves more skepticism than the abstract grants it.

baselines

Clean within-family base→SFT sweeps across 3 families and a 10× size range. The key LIMA-1k vs Task-1k contrast is fair as a methodology demo, but it's a generalist dataset judged on task benchmarks — illustrative, not a head-to-head of equally-targeted methods.

contamination

Genuinely addressed for math via a GSM1k held-out twin (App. A.1) — the right control. Not done for coding, IFEval, or multihop, where 2024-era contamination is plausible.

what's missed

No R²/error bars/multi-seed; power law fit on ~5–6 points over one decade of data, where it's hard to distinguish from log or saturating curves. 70B curves are sparse. Win-rate critique uses one LLM judge, not humans. Facts100 is N=100, GPT-4o-scored.

would raise confidence

Multi-seed runs with CIs and reported fit quality vs alternative functional forms; held-out contamination twins for code/IF; a human win-rate study; a larger, human-validated Facts100; and a look at multi-task degradation and the RLHF stage.

// bottom line

A small, careful study that lands a big methodological punch: measure post-training on objective benchmarks and the "alignment is superficial" story collapses into "style is superficial, capability is not." The functional-form claim (a clean power law) is the soft spot; the qualitative claim — that win-rate evals hide real reasoning gains and that data still buys capability well past a thousand examples — is the part worth carrying forward.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. Post-training scales like pre-training, in examples. Task accuracy follows P ∝ D1/b in the number of finetuning examples across Llama-3, Mistral and Llama-2 — it keeps rising well past the "1,000 examples is enough" regime, so curated data budgets still buy capability.
  2. "Superficial" applies to style, not skill. Formatting errors vanish in ~100 examples, but total errors track reasoning errors (r² = 0.98 math / 0.99 multihop on Llama-3-8B) and keep falling with more data. Don't mistake a well-formatted answer for a correct one.
  3. Win-rate can crown the worse reasoner. A style-tuned model won 84.4% of GPT-4o head-to-heads on GSM8k while solving only 14.7% of problems, vs 46.5%-accurate model winning 0.24%. Pair preference evals with objective, task-specific benchmarks — and good reasoning post-training even lets a model use post-cutoff knowledge it's handed.
